verses.text: With Saul dead, David does not seize the throne but asks the LORD, 'Shall I go up?' and where. Guided to Hebron, he is anointed king over Judah. The man who waited years for the crown still will not move a step without God. Patience that waited now asks before it acts.

With Saul dead, David does not seize the throne but asks the LORD, 'Shall I go up?' and where. Guided to Hebron, he is anointed king over Judah. The man who waited years for the crown still will not move a step without God. Patience that waited now asks before it acts.

With Saul dead, David does not seize the throne but asks the LORD, 'Shall I go up?' and where. Guided to Hebron, he is anointed king over Judah. The man who waited years for the crown still will not move a step without God. Patience that waited now asks before it acts.

With Saul dead, David does not seize the throne but asks the LORD, 'Shall I go up?' and where. Guided to Hebron, he is anointed king over Judah. The man who waited years for the crown still will not move a step without God. Patience that waited now asks before it acts.

David's first royal act is to thank the men of Jabesh-gilead for honouring Saul's body, and to bless them. He praises kindness shown to his old enemy. A gracious king notices and rewards faithfulness, even faithfulness to the rival he is replacing.

But Abner sets up Saul's surviving son Ish-bosheth as a rival king over the rest of Israel, and David reigns over Judah alone for seven and a half years. God's promise is sure, yet its full coming is delayed by men's ambition. The kingdom is given by stages, not all at once.

But Abner sets up Saul's surviving son Ish-bosheth as a rival king over the rest of Israel, and David reigns over Judah alone for seven and a half years. God's promise is sure, yet its full coming is delayed by men's ambition. The kingdom is given by stages, not all at once.

The two sides meet at the pool of Gibeon, and Abner proposes that twelve young men from each side 'play before us.' What looks like a contest of champions is a deadly game. Pride dresses up bloodshed as sport, but the LORD is not pleased with strife among brothers.

The two sides meet at the pool of Gibeon, and Abner proposes that twelve young men from each side 'play before us.' What looks like a contest of champions is a deadly game. Pride dresses up bloodshed as sport, but the LORD is not pleased with strife among brothers.

The two sides meet at the pool of Gibeon, and Abner proposes that twelve young men from each side 'play before us.' What looks like a contest of champions is a deadly game. Pride dresses up bloodshed as sport, but the LORD is not pleased with strife among brothers.

The twelve pairs grab one another and stab, falling together, and a fierce battle follows in which Abner's men are beaten. The reckless 'play' turns into slaughter. Conflicts entered lightly seldom end lightly; what pride starts, many graves must finish.

The twelve pairs grab one another and stab, falling together, and a fierce battle follows in which Abner's men are beaten. The reckless 'play' turns into slaughter. Conflicts entered lightly seldom end lightly; what pride starts, many graves must finish.

Swift-footed Asahel, Joab's brother, fixes on Abner and chases him, refusing to turn aside right or left. Courage without wisdom can be fatal. To pursue a battle-hardened foe out of zeal alone, deaf to caution, is to run headlong toward one's own ruin.

Swift-footed Asahel, Joab's brother, fixes on Abner and chases him, refusing to turn aside right or left. Courage without wisdom can be fatal. To pursue a battle-hardened foe out of zeal alone, deaf to caution, is to run headlong toward one's own ruin.

Abner twice warns Asahel to turn away and take other spoil, unwilling to kill Joab's brother, but Asahel will not stop, and Abner strikes him dead with the butt of his spear. Mercy refused becomes blood spilled. Stubbornness can turn a generous warning into a grave.

Abner twice warns Asahel to turn away and take other spoil, unwilling to kill Joab's brother, but Asahel will not stop, and Abner strikes him dead with the butt of his spear. Mercy refused becomes blood spilled. Stubbornness can turn a generous warning into a grave.

Abner twice warns Asahel to turn away and take other spoil, unwilling to kill Joab's brother, but Asahel will not stop, and Abner strikes him dead with the butt of his spear. Mercy refused becomes blood spilled. Stubbornness can turn a generous warning into a grave.

Joab and Abishai pursue till evening, but Abner pleads, 'shall the sword devour for ever?', and Joab at last sounds the retreat. Even hardened soldiers can be brought to pause the killing. There is a time to press a fight and a time to let brothers stop and go home.

The tally of the dead is counted — far more of Abner's men than David's — and Asahel is carried home and buried at Bethlehem. War leaves grief on every side. Scripture does not glory in the body count but names the cost, that we may hate the strife brothers wage.
